# Async I/O and tokio-util Parity Audit

**Bead**: `asupersync-2oh2u.2.1` ([T2.1])
**Generated by**: SapphireHill (claude-code / opus-4.6)
**Date**: 2026-03-03
**Dependencies**: T1.3.c (roadmap baseline)
**Purpose**: Function-level parity inventory versus `tokio::io` and `tokio-util::io/codec` surfaces. Covers semantics that matter for migration: EOF behavior, split ownership, vectored I/O, shutdown, and buffering invariants.

---

## 1. Scope

This audit covers two Tokio crate surfaces:

| Tokio Surface | Asupersync Modules | Total LOC |
|---------------|-------------------|-----------|
| `tokio::io` | `src/io/` (read, write, copy, split, buf_reader, buf_writer, lines, seek, read_buf, write_permit, ext/) | ~5,800 |
| `tokio-util::codec` | `src/codec/` (decoder, encoder, framed, framed_read, framed_write, length_delimited, lines, bytes_codec) | ~1,900 |
| `tokio-util::io` adapters | `src/stream/` (partial) | ~500 |
| **Total** | | **~8,200** |

Asupersync-specific extensions (IoCap, BrowserStream, BrowserStorage) are noted but not counted toward parity.

---

## 2. Core Trait Parity

### 2.1 tokio::io Traits

| Tokio Trait | Asupersync | Location | Status | Notes |
|-------------|-----------|----------|--------|-------|
| `AsyncRead` | `AsyncRead` | `io/read.rs` | Complete | Same `poll_read(Pin, Cx, ReadBuf)` signature |
| `AsyncWrite` | `AsyncWrite` | `io/write.rs` | Complete | Same 4-method contract (write, vectored, flush, shutdown) |
| `AsyncBufRead` | `AsyncBufRead` | `io/copy.rs` | Complete | `poll_fill_buf` + `consume` |
| `AsyncSeek` | `AsyncSeek` | `io/seek.rs` | Complete | `poll_seek(Pin, Cx, SeekFrom)` |

**Semantic Equivalence**: All four traits match Tokio signatures. `ReadBuf` wrapper is simplified (assumes initialized buffers, no `MaybeUninit` optimization).

### 2.2 tokio-util Traits

| tokio-util Trait | Asupersync | Location | Status |
|-----------------|-----------|----------|--------|
| `Decoder` | `Decoder` | `codec/decoder.rs` | Complete |
| `Encoder<Item>` | `Encoder<Item>` | `codec/encoder.rs` | Complete |

---

## 3. AsyncReadExt Method Parity

| Tokio Method | Asupersync | Status | Gap ID |
|-------------|-----------|--------|--------|
| `read(&mut buf)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G1 |
| `read_buf(&mut buf)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G2 |
| `read_exact(&mut buf)` | `read_exact` | Complete | ‚Äî |
| `read_to_end(&mut vec)` | `read_to_end` | Complete | ‚Äî |
| `read_to_string(&mut s)` | `read_to_string` | Complete | ‚Äî |
| `read_u8()` | `read_u8` | Complete | ‚Äî |
| `read_i8()` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G3 |
| `read_u16()` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G3 |
| `read_u16_le()` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G3 |
| `read_u32()` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G3 |
| `read_u32_le()` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G3 |
| `read_u64()` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G3 |
| `read_u64_le()` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G3 |
| `read_u128()` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G3 |
| `read_u128_le()` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G3 |
| `read_i16()` ... `read_i128_le()` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G3 |
| `read_f32()` / `read_f64()` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G3 |
| `chain(next)` | `chain` | Complete | ‚Äî |
| `take(limit)` | `take` | Complete | ‚Äî |

### IO-G1: `read()` ‚Äî Low Priority
Missing basic `read(&mut [u8]) -> usize` wrapper. Users must use `poll_read` directly or `read_exact`. Low priority because `read_exact` is the safer, more common pattern.

### IO-G2: `read_buf()` ‚Äî Medium Priority
Missing `read_buf` that works with `BufMut` trait. Limits interop with `bytes` crate patterns.

### IO-G3: Integer/Float Read Methods ‚Äî Medium Priority
Missing 20+ typed read methods (`read_u16`, `read_u32`, `read_u64`, `read_i16`, etc. in both big-endian and little-endian variants). These are heavily used in wire protocol implementations. Currently, database/messaging wire protocol code handles endianness manually.

---

## 4. AsyncWriteExt Method Parity

| Tokio Method | Asupersync | Status | Gap ID |
|-------------|-----------|--------|--------|
| `write(&buf)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G4 |
| `write_vectored(&bufs)` | `write_vectored` | Complete | ‚Äî |
| `write_buf(&mut buf)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G5 |
| `write_all(&buf)` | `write_all` | Complete | ‚Äî |
| `write_all_buf(&mut buf)` | `write_all_buf` | Complete | ‚Äî |
| `write_u8(n)` | `write_u8` | Complete | ‚Äî |
| `write_i8(n)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G6 |
| `write_u16(n)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G6 |
| `write_u16_le(n)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G6 |
| `write_u32(n)` ... `write_u128_le(n)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G6 |
| `write_i16(n)` ... `write_i128_le(n)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G6 |
| `write_f32(n)` / `write_f64(n)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G6 |
| `flush()` | `flush` | Complete | ‚Äî |
| `shutdown()` | `shutdown` | Complete | ‚Äî |

### IO-G4: `write()` ‚Äî Low Priority
Missing basic `write(&[u8]) -> usize` wrapper. Same rationale as IO-G1.

### IO-G5: `write_buf()` ‚Äî Medium Priority
Missing `write_buf` for `Buf` trait. Asupersync has its own `Buf` trait but no `write_buf` method.

### IO-G6: Integer/Float Write Methods ‚Äî Medium Priority
Same scope as IO-G3 but for writes. Missing 20+ typed write methods.

## 5. AsyncBufReadExt Method Parity

| Tokio Method | Asupersync | Status | Gap ID |
|-------------|-----------|--------|--------|
| `read_until(byte, buf)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G7 |
| `read_line(buf)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G7 |
| `split(byte)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G7 |
| `fill_buf()` | `poll_fill_buf` (poll-based) | Complete | ‚Äî |
| `consume(amt)` | `consume` | Complete | ‚Äî |
| `lines()` | `Lines` stream | Complete | ‚Äî |

### IO-G7: AsyncBufReadExt Convenience Methods ‚Äî Medium Priority
Missing `read_until`, `read_line`, and `split(byte)` convenience methods. The `Lines` iterator covers the most common case.

---

## 6. I/O Utility Type Parity

### 6.1 Buffered I/O

| Tokio Type | Asupersync | Status | Notes |
|-----------|-----------|--------|-------|
| `BufReader<R>` | `BufReader<R>` | Complete | Same semantics, 8192 default |
| `BufWriter<W>` | `BufWriter<W>` | Complete | Same semantics, 8192 default |
| `BufStream<RW>` | ‚Äî | Missing (IO-G8) | Combines BufReader + BufWriter |

### 6.2 Split

| Tokio Type | Asupersync | Status | Gap ID |
|-----------|-----------|--------|--------|
| `split(stream) ‚Üí (ReadHalf, WriteHalf)` | `SplitStream::split()` | Different | IO-G9 |
| `into_split() ‚Üí (OwnedReadHalf, OwnedWriteHalf)` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G9 |

### IO-G9: Split Ownership Model ‚Äî High Priority

**Tokio** provides two split modes:
- `split(&mut stream)` ‚Äî borrowed halves, same task only
- `into_split(stream)` ‚Äî owned halves via `Arc<Mutex>`, can be sent to different tasks

**Asupersync** provides only:
- `SplitStream::split()` ‚Äî `RefCell`-based, panics on concurrent access

**Migration blocker**: Any Tokio code using `into_split()` to send read/write halves to separate tasks will not work. This is a common pattern in bidirectional protocols (WebSocket, gRPC streams, database replication).

### 6.3 Adapters

| Tokio/tokio-util Type | Asupersync | Status | Gap ID |
|----------------------|-----------|--------|--------|
| `Empty` (zero-length reader) | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G10 |
| `Repeat(byte)` (infinite reader) | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G10 |
| `Sink` (discard writer) | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G10 |
| `Duplex` (in-memory bidirectional) | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G11 |
| `SimplexStream` (in-memory unidirectional) | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G11 |
| `ReaderStream<R>` (AsyncRead ‚Üí Stream) | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G12 |
| `StreamReader<S>` (Stream ‚Üí AsyncRead) | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G12 |
| `SinkWriter<S>` (Sink ‚Üí AsyncWrite) | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G12 |
| `CopyToBytes` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G13 |
| `InspectReader<R, F>` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G13 |
| `InspectWriter<W, F>` | ‚Äî | Missing | IO-G13 |

### IO-G10: Zero/Infinite Adapters ‚Äî Low Priority
`Empty`, `Repeat`, `Sink` are trivial to implement (< 50 lines each) and used mainly in tests.

### IO-G11: In-Memory Duplex/Simplex ‚Äî High Priority
`DuplexStream` and `SimplexStream` are essential for testing. Without `DuplexStream`, integration tests for bidirectional protocols require real sockets or custom mocks.

### IO-G12: Stream/AsyncRead Bridge Adapters ‚Äî High Priority
`ReaderStream`, `StreamReader`, and `SinkWriter` bridge the `Stream` and `AsyncRead/AsyncWrite` worlds. These are used extensively in middleware (e.g., HTTP body streaming, gRPC streaming).

### IO-G13: Inspection Adapters ‚Äî Low Priority
`CopyToBytes`, `InspectReader`, `InspectWriter` are convenience wrappers. Nice to have for debugging.

---

## 7. Codec Parity

### 7.1 Built-In Codecs

| tokio-util Codec | Asupersync | Status |
|-----------------|-----------|--------|
| `BytesCodec` | `BytesCodec` | Complete |
| `LinesCodec` | `LinesCodec` | Complete |
| `LengthDelimitedCodec` | `LengthDelimitedCodec` | Complete |
| `AnyDelimiterCodec` | ‚Äî | Missing (IO-G14) |

### IO-G14: AnyDelimiterCodec ‚Äî Low Priority
Generalized delimiter-based codec. Niche use case; `LinesCodec` covers `\n` and `LengthDelimitedCodec` covers framed protocols.

### 7.2 Framed Transport

| tokio-util Type | Asupersync | Status |
|----------------|-----------|--------|
| `Framed<T, U>` | `Framed<T, U>` | Complete |
| `FramedRead<R, D>` | `FramedRead<R, D>` | Complete |
| `FramedWrite<W, E>` | `FramedWrite<W, E>` | Complete |
| `FramedParts<T, U>` | `FramedParts<T, U>` | Complete |

---

## 8. Semantic Differences

### 8.1 EOF Behavior

| Behavior | Tokio | Asupersync | Status |
|----------|-------|-----------|--------|
| `read()` returns 0 on EOF | Yes | Yes (via ReadBuf) | Equivalent |
| `read_exact` errors on early EOF | `UnexpectedEof` | `UnexpectedEof` | Equivalent |
| `read_to_end` stops on EOF | Yes | Yes | Equivalent |
| Decoder `decode_eof` called at EOF | Yes | Yes | Equivalent |
| BufReader returns empty slice at EOF | Yes | Yes | Equivalent |

### 8.2 Shutdown Semantics

| Behavior | Tokio | Asupersync | Status |
|----------|-------|-----------|--------|
| `poll_shutdown` flushes before close | Implementation-defined | Implementation-defined | Equivalent |
| BufWriter flush on shutdown | Yes | Yes | Equivalent |
| TLS shutdown (alert + close) | Via tokio-native-tls/rustls | Via acceptor/connector | Equivalent |
| Split half shutdown | Depends on impl | N/A (RefCell-based) | Gap (IO-G9) |

### 8.3 Buffering Invariants

| Invariant | Tokio | Asupersync | Status |
|-----------|-------|-----------|--------|
| BufReader default capacity | 8192 | 8192 | Equivalent |
| BufWriter default capacity | 8192 | 8192 | Equivalent |
| BufWriter auto-flush on full | Yes | Yes | Equivalent |
| BufWriter data loss on drop without flush | Yes (documented) | Yes (documented) | Equivalent |
| Copy buffer size | 8192 | 8192 | Equivalent |

### 8.4 Vectored I/O

| Behavior | Tokio | Asupersync | Status |
|----------|-------|-----------|--------|
| `is_write_vectored()` capability check | Yes | Yes | Equivalent |
| Default vectored ‚Üí single buffer fallback | Yes | Yes | Equivalent |
| Separate `AsyncReadVectored` trait | No (part of AsyncRead) | Yes (separate trait) | Different |

**Note**: Asupersync separates vectored I/O into its own trait (`AsyncReadVectored`, `AsyncWriteVectored`). This is a deliberate design choice for capability-based I/O but means implementors must implement both traits.

### 8.5 Cancel-Safety

| Operation | Tokio | Asupersync | Notes |
|-----------|-------|-----------|-------|
| `read_exact` | Not cancel-safe | Not cancel-safe | Consistent |
| `write_all` | Not cancel-safe | Not cancel-safe | Consistent |
| `copy` | Cancel-safe | Cancel-safe | Consistent |
| `flush` | Cancel-safe | Cancel-safe | Consistent |
| `BufReader::fill_buf` | Cancel-safe | Cancel-safe | Consistent |

Asupersync adds `WritePermit` for explicit two-phase cancel-safe writes ‚Äî no Tokio equivalent.

---

## 9. Gap Summary

| ID | Description | Severity | Effort | Phase |
|----|-------------|----------|--------|-------|
| IO-G1 | `read()` basic method | Low | <1 day | D |
| IO-G2 | `read_buf()` with BufMut | Medium | 1-2 days | C |
| IO-G3 | Integer/float read methods (20+) | Medium | 2-3 days | B |
| IO-G4 | `write()` basic method | Low | <1 day | D |
| IO-G5 | `write_buf()` with Buf | Medium | 1-2 days | C |
| IO-G6 | Integer/float write methods (20+) | Medium | 2-3 days | B |
| IO-G7 | AsyncBufReadExt convenience | Medium | 1-2 days | C |
| IO-G8 | BufStream combined type | Low | <1 day | D |
| IO-G9 | Arc-based split (into_split) | High | 3-5 days | A |
| IO-G10 | Empty/Repeat/Sink adapters | Low | <1 day | D |
| IO-G11 | DuplexStream/SimplexStream | High | 2-3 days | A |
| IO-G12 | ReaderStream/StreamReader/SinkWriter | High | 3-5 days | A |
| IO-G13 | Inspect/CopyToBytes adapters | Low | 1-2 days | D |
| IO-G14 | AnyDelimiterCodec | Low | <1 day | D |

**Total**: 14 gaps (3 High, 5 Medium, 6 Low)

### Priority Ranking

**Phase A ‚Äî Critical for Migration** (IO-G9, IO-G11, IO-G12):
- `into_split()` blocks bidirectional protocol migration
- `DuplexStream` blocks integration testing
- Stream/AsyncRead bridges block middleware migration

**Phase B ‚Äî Needed for Wire Protocols** (IO-G3, IO-G6):
- Integer read/write methods used extensively in protocol implementations
- Currently handled manually in database/messaging wire code

**Phase C ‚Äî Convenience** (IO-G2, IO-G5, IO-G7):
- Useful but workarounds exist

**Phase D ‚Äî Polish** (IO-G1, IO-G4, IO-G8, IO-G10, IO-G13, IO-G14):
- Trivial or niche

---

## 10. Asupersync-Specific Extensions (No Tokio Equivalent)

| Feature | Location | Purpose |
|---------|----------|---------|
| `WritePermit` | `io/write_permit.rs` | Two-phase cancel-safe writes |
| `IoCap` trait | `io/cap.rs` | Capability-based I/O access control |
| `BrowserStream` | `io/browser_stream.rs` | WASM ReadableStream/WritableStream bridge |
| `BrowserStorage` | `io/browser_storage.rs` | WASM localStorage/IndexedDB bridge |
| `CopyWithProgress` | `io/copy.rs` | Copy with progress callback |
| `CopyBidirectional` | `io/copy.rs` | Full-duplex copy |
| `AsyncReadVectored` (separate trait) | `io/read.rs` | Explicit vectored I/O capability |
| `AsyncWriteVectored` (separate trait) | `io/write.rs` | Explicit vectored I/O capability |
